text:
Lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) represent the leading non-viral platform for nucleic acid delivery, yet the specific influence of their physicochemical properties on performance following intramuscular (IM) injection remains poorly understood compared to intravenous routes. This study systematically evaluates how particle size, surface charge, and PEGylated lipid composition dictate the transfection efficiency and biodistribution of messenger RNA (mRNA) encapsulated within LNPs. Using luciferase mRNA (mLuc) as a reporter, the researchers synthesized a library of LNPs with varying diameters, lipid-based charges, and PEG lipid architectures.

Experimental results demonstrated that smaller LNP diameters and neutral surface potentials significantly enhanced transfection efficiency both in vitro and in vivo. Intriguingly, while these parameters governed protein expression levels, they did not substantially alter the anatomical distribution of the nanoparticles after IM administration. Further investigation into the role of PEGylated lipids revealed that molecules with shorter acyl chains (e.g., DMG-PEG2k) facilitated superior transfection by promoting more efficient cellular uptake and endosomal escape. Mechanistic studies using diverse endocytosis inhibitors indicated that the length of the PEG lipid acyl chain fundamentally shifts the cellular internalisation pathway—primarily between clathrin-mediated endocytosis and macropinocytosis—thereby influencing the intracellular fate of the cargo. By elucidating the interplay between LNP architecture and physiological behavior in muscle tissue, these findings offer critical design principles for optimizing the next generation of mRNA vaccines and therapeutics.
